title: Total Sales stepped up sharply in 2021 versus 2020 across matched months
description: Monthly Total Sales were higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020, indicating a broad-based year-over-year growth trend rather than a one-off spike. Step 4 explicitly identifies this as a full-calendar pattern, and Step 3 supports it with the structural link between Units Sold and Total Sales (correlation 0.913), suggesting the sales lift was reinforced by stronger volume.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Total Sales sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020.' Step 3: Units Sold vs Total Sales correlation = 0.9134314656070663.
data_evidence.data_points: 2021 monthly Total Sales exceed the corresponding 2020 month in all matched months.

title: Operating Profit followed the same year-over-year expansion pattern as sales
description: Monthly Operating Profit was also higher in every month of 2021 than in the same month of 2020, showing that profit growth kept pace with revenue growth. Step 4 flags this as a consistent monthly pattern, while Step 3 shows a very strong Total Sales-to-Operating Profit relationship of 0.956, indicating that the revenue expansion translated efficiently into profit dollars.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Operating Profit sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020.' Step 3: Total Sales vs Operating Profit correlation = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: All matched 2021 months show higher Operating Profit than 2020 counterparts.

title: Units Sold rose materially in 2021, with every matched month above 2020
description: Monthly unit volume increased consistently from 2020 to 2021 across all matched months, pointing to stronger demand and/or broader transaction activity. Step 4 identifies this as a universal year-over-year pattern, and Step 3 shows Units Sold is strongly tied to both Total Sales (0.913) and Operating Profit (0.892), making this volume trend commercially important.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Units Sold sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than 2020.' Step 3: Units Sold vs Total Sales = 0.9134314656070663; Units Sold vs Operating Profit = 0.8923794236295555.
data_evidence.data_points: Each matched month in 2021 records higher Units Sold than the same month in 2020.

title: 2021 unit volume built toward a late-summer peak before an autumn dip
description: Units Sold in 2021 were lowest in early spring, rose into late summer, then declined in autumn before recovering in December. This indicates demand was not evenly distributed through the year and instead concentrated more heavily around the summer selling period. Step 4 identifies this exact seasonal pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: '2021 Units Sold show summer peak and fall dip.'
data_evidence.data_points: Pattern by period: early spring low, late summer high, autumn decline, December recovery.

title: Average selling price fell to an early-2021 low, then climbed steadily into late 2021
description: Price per Unit was relatively higher in parts of 2020, dropped to its lowest levels in early 2021, and then trended upward through late 2021. This suggests the 2021 growth story was initially volume-led, with pricing power improving later in the year. Step 4 provides the trend shape, while Step 2 notes only 0.84% of Price per Unit records are outliers, so the pattern is less likely to be driven by widespread pricing anomalies.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Price per Unit mean is lower in early 2021 than in 2020, then trends upward through late 2021.' Step 2: Price per Unit outliers = 81 records (0.84%).
data_evidence.data_points: Relative pattern: higher in parts of 2020, lowest in early 2021, then rising through late 2021.

title: Transaction activity expanded sharply in 2021, even as units per record became smaller on average
description: Step 4 shows monthly record counts rose sharply from 2020 to 2021 and stayed consistently high throughout 2021, while average Units Sold per record were generally higher in 2020 than in 2021. This means growth came from many more transactions, not just larger baskets. The pattern is operationally important because it implies higher sales throughput with smaller average order sizes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Sharp year-over-year step-up in transaction volume in 2021' and 'Average Units Sold per record are higher in 2020 than in 2021.'
data_evidence.data_points: 2021 has consistently higher monthly record counts, while mean Units Sold per record is generally lower than in 2020.

title: Online channel likely participated in the broad 2021 sales step-up seen across the business
description: The dataset shows a business-wide pattern where monthly Total Sales in 2021 exceeded the same month in 2020 across the full calendar. For the Online channel, this implies the same directional trend is highly relevant to monitor, especially because the overall increase was consistent rather than isolated to a few months. This segment-specific view is important for channel planning even though the evidence comes from the overall temporal pattern in Step 4.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: 'Total Sales sums are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020.'
data_evidence.data_points: Global monthly pattern indicates a persistent 2021 uplift that should be tracked within Online specifically.

title: West region should be monitored for the same summer-peak and fall-softening sales pattern seen overall in 2021
description: At the overall level, 2021 Total Sales declined from January to March, rose through July, softened through October, and rebounded in November-December. Applying this trend lens to the West region is valuable because it highlights when regional demand planning and inventory pressure are most likely to intensify or ease. The insight is segment-focused, grounded in the Step 4 business-wide seasonal pattern.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4: '2021 Total Sales dip in Q1, strengthen through summer, soften in fall, rebound in December.'
data_evidence.data_points: Global 2021 sequence: Jan→Mar down, Mar→Jul up, Jul→Oct down, Nov→Dec up; this is the key pattern to test and manage in West.

title: Operating Profit has the largest concentration of extreme records
description: Operating Profit shows 706 outlier records, representing 7.32% of the dataset, which is the highest outlier share among the core financial measures reviewed. This indicates unusually large or small profit records are common enough to materially distort average profitability and segment comparisons.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 706 Operating Profit outliers; 7.32% of all records; severity marked high.
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier rate exceeds Total Sales (6.77%), Units Sold (5.27%), Price per Unit (0.84%), and Operating Margin (0.46%).

title: Total Sales contains an unusually high number of extreme revenue records
description: Total Sales has 653 outlier records, or 6.77% of all observations, making extreme revenue events a recurring anomaly rather than a handful of isolated cases. Given Total Sales is a primary business KPI, this level of abnormality can noticeably skew revenue reporting and benchmarking.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 653 Total Sales outliers; 6.77% of records; severity marked high.
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales outlier share is the second highest among reviewed numerical measures.

title: Units Sold shows frequent volume spikes relative to the rest of the data
description: Units Sold includes 508 outlier records, equal to 5.27% of the dataset. This means roughly 1 in 19 records has an unusually high or low unit quantity, suggesting repeated bulk-order, return, or data-entry anomalies that can distort demand and inventory analysis.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 508 Units Sold outliers; 5.27% of records; severity marked high.
data_evidence.data_points: Units Sold outlier frequency is lower than Operating Profit and Total Sales, but still materially high for a core operational metric.

title: Revenue and profit are dominated by a small set of exceptionally large transactions
description: Step 3 reports extreme right skew and large mean-median gaps for both Total Sales and Operating Profit, indicating that a relatively small number of very large records sit far above the bulk of transactions. This is a classic outlier pattern where a few oversized deals disproportionately drive aggregate performance.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Both Total Sales and Operating Profit are described as extremely right-skewed with large mean-median gaps.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 explicitly states revenue and profit are highly concentrated in a relatively small number of large records.

title: Price per Unit anomalies are rare but still meaningful in a critical pricing field
description: Only 81 records, or 0.84% of the dataset, are outliers in Price per Unit. While the rate is much lower than for sales, profit, or volume, pricing is a root driver of both revenue and margin, so even this small set of abnormal prices may signal discounting errors, unit inconsistencies, or exceptional premium pricing.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 81 Price per Unit outliers; 0.84% of records; severity marked medium.
data_evidence.data_points: Price per Unit has the second-lowest outlier count among the reviewed numerical columns.

title: Operating Margin is unusually stable compared with dollar-based measures
description: Operating Margin has just 44 outlier records, or 0.46% of observations, far below Operating Profit at 7.32% and Total Sales at 6.77%. This gap suggests the main anomalies are driven by transaction size and volume rather than widespread instability in percentage profitability.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers: 44 records (0.46%) versus Operating Profit 706 (7.32%) and Total Sales 653 (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 also notes Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures.

title: The West region likely contains a disproportionate share of high-value anomalies
description: The dataset shows strong structural links between Units Sold, Total Sales, and Operating Profit, with correlations of 0.913, 0.956, and 0.892 respectively, while Step 2 flags 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers. In a high-scale region such as the West, these relationships make extreme revenue records especially likely to coincide with extreme profit records, creating concentrated high-value anomalies worth validating first.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation is 0.913 between Units Sold and Total Sales, 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, and 0.892 between Units Sold and Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 separately identifies 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers, implying extreme records likely cluster across these linked measures.

title: Online sales should be prioritized for anomaly review because extreme volume can cascade into revenue and profit outliers
description: Step 2 identifies 508 Units Sold outliers, 653 Total Sales outliers, and 706 Operating Profit outliers, while Step 3 shows these measures are tightly correlated at 0.913, 0.956, and 0.892. In the Online channel, this means unusually large order quantities are especially likely to propagate into outsized sales and profit records, making it a natural subspace for anomaly triage.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold–Total Sales correlation 0.913; Total Sales–Operating Profit correlation 0.956; Units Sold–Operating Profit correlation 0.892.
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier counts are 508 for Units Sold, 653 for Total Sales, and 706 for Operating Profit.

title: Units Sold and Total Sales move together very strongly across the dataset
description: There is a very strong positive correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales, with a correlation coefficient of 0.913. This indicates that records with higher unit volume almost always generate higher revenue, making sales volume the clearest driver of revenue variation in the dataset. Step 3 also notes this is a structural relationship, so it should be interpreted as expected business linkage rather than independent causal evidence.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales) = 0.9134314656070663
data_evidence.data_points: Listed among the strongest correlations in Step 3.

title: Total Sales and Operating Profit are almost perfectly aligned
description: Total Sales and Operating Profit have the strongest relationship in the analysis, with a correlation of 0.956. This near-linear association means higher-revenue records tend to translate directly into higher operating profit. Step 3 also notes that Operating Margin is comparatively stable, which helps explain why profit rises so consistently with sales dollars.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.9563074748281619
data_evidence.data_points: This is the highest correlation reported in Step 3.

title: Higher unit volume is also strongly associated with higher operating profit
description: Units Sold and Operating Profit show a strong positive correlation of 0.892. This suggests that larger-quantity transactions are typically more profitable in absolute dollars, even if margin percentages remain relatively stable. In practical terms, volume expansion appears closely tied to profit growth across records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.8923794236295555
data_evidence.data_points: Reported as one of the three strongest correlations in Step 3.

title: In the West region, sales and profit likely remain tightly linked because margin is stable while dollar measures scale together
description: For the West region, the most relevant correlation pattern is the same core global relationship: Total Sales and Operating Profit move together very closely, supported by the overall correlation of 0.956. Step 3 adds that Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures, implying that within a major region like West, larger sales records should also correspond to larger profit records with limited margin disruption.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619; Operating Margin described as much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies Total Sales and Operating Profit as the strongest pair overall.

title: Online sales likely preserve the same strong volume-to-revenue relationship seen overall
description: Within the Online channel, the most business-relevant correlation is the strong alignment between Units Sold and Total Sales, anchored by the overall dataset correlation of 0.913. Because Total Sales is fundamentally volume-driven and Step 3 explicitly flags this as a structural relationship, the Online segment is a natural place to expect the same strong positive association between unit growth and revenue growth.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.9134314656070663
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies Units Sold and Total Sales as one of the strongest relationships and describes it as structurally driven.

title: Total Sales is heavily right-skewed, with a small share of records contributing disproportionately large revenue
description: The distribution of Total Sales is strongly concentrated in lower-value transactions with a long right tail of very large records. Step 2 flagged 653 outliers, representing 6.77% of records, and Step 3 noted a large mean-median gap and extreme right skew. This means a relatively small portion of transactions is driving an outsized share of revenue, so average sales values are likely inflated versus the typical transaction.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales has extreme right skew with a large mean-median gap; 653 outliers were detected in Step 2 (6.77% of records).
data_evidence.data_points: 653 Total Sales outliers; 6.77% of all records.

title: Operating Profit is even more concentrated than typical transactions suggest
description: Operating Profit shows a pronounced right-skewed distribution, indicating that most records generate modest profit while a smaller set of transactions contributes very large profit amounts. Step 2 identified 706 outliers, equal to 7.32% of records, and Step 3 highlighted a substantial mean-median gap. This suggests profit comparisons based on averages alone may overstate normal transaction performance.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit is extremely right-skewed with a large mean-median gap; Step 2 found 706 outliers.
data_evidence.data_points: 706 Operating Profit outliers; 7.32% of records.

title: Units Sold has a wide spread, with high-volume transactions standing out from the bulk of records
description: The Units Sold distribution is uneven, with most transactions clustered at lower volumes and a smaller group of very large-quantity sales extending the upper tail. Step 2 detected 508 outliers, or 5.27% of records, while Step 3 showed Units Sold is strongly tied to Total Sales (correlation 0.913). This indicates that unusually large orders are an important driver of revenue variation across records.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold contains 508 outliers and is strongly correlated with Total Sales in Step 3.
data_evidence.data_points: 508 Units Sold outliers; 5.27% of records; correlation with Total Sales = 0.913.

title: Operating Margin is far more stable than revenue and profit dollars
description: Compared with Total Sales and Operating Profit, Operating Margin has a much tighter distribution. Step 2 found only 44 outliers, just 0.46% of records, versus 653 Total Sales outliers and 706 Operating Profit outliers. Step 3 also concluded that margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures, implying that business differences are driven more by transaction size and volume than by large swings in percentage profitability.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar measures; Step 2 outlier rates are far lower for margin.
data_evidence.data_points: Operating Margin outliers: 44 (0.46%); Total Sales outliers: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers: 706 (7.32%).

title: Price per Unit is comparatively consistent, with fewer extreme pricing records than sales or profit measures
description: Price per Unit appears much less dispersed than transaction-level sales and profit outcomes. Step 2 identified only 81 outliers, or 0.84% of records, compared with 508 outliers in Units Sold, 653 in Total Sales, and 706 in Operating Profit. This suggests pricing is relatively controlled, while variation in revenue and profit is driven more by order size and mix than by frequent extreme prices.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit has a much lower outlier rate than volume, revenue, and profit measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Price per Unit outliers: 81 (0.84%); Units Sold: 508 (5.27%); Total Sales: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit: 706 (7.32%).

title: Online sales likely show stronger concentration in large transactions than a typical channel view would imply
description: Within the Online channel, the business should expect a distribution where a relatively small number of large transactions can materially affect reported performance. This segment-specific caution is supported by the global pattern in Step 3 showing extreme right skew in Total Sales and Operating Profit, along with Step 2 outlier rates of 6.77% for Total Sales and 7.32% for Operating Profit. For Online reporting, median-based comparisons would likely be more representative than mean-only summaries.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global revenue and profit measures are highly right-skewed, with large mean-median gaps and elevated outlier counts.
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales outliers: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers: 706 (7.32%).

title: California transactions should be evaluated with care because high-value records can dominate state-level averages
description: For California, average sales and profit metrics are likely sensitive to a small number of unusually large transactions. This follows the overall dataset pattern from Step 3, where Total Sales and Operating Profit are both highly right-skewed, and Step 2 found 653 and 706 outliers respectively. In a major market such as California, comparing medians or distribution spreads would provide a more balanced view than relying only on averages.

data_evidence.key_statistics: High-value revenue and profit outliers are common enough to distort segment averages.
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales outliers: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers: 706 (7.32%).

title: Revenue varies more across records than profitability percentage does
description: Comparing the distributions directly, Total Sales and Operating Profit have much heavier tails than Operating Margin. Step 2 shows outlier rates above 6% for both dollar measures, versus just 0.46% for margin, and Step 3 explicitly states that margin is much more stable. This means transaction-to-transaction differences are much larger in absolute dollars than in percentage profitability, which is important when benchmarking performance across products, retailers, or regions.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin is stable relative to Total Sales and Operating Profit, which are both highly skewed.
data_evidence.data_points: Outlier rates: Total Sales 6.77%, Operating Profit 7.32%, Operating Margin 0.46%.

title: Sales and profit both follow the same scale-driven pattern across the dataset
description: The business shows a consistent pattern where larger sales records also produce larger profits. Step 3 reports a very strong correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, indicating that profit rises almost in lockstep with revenue across transactions. This aligns with Step 4, which also found that monthly Total Sales and Operating Profit move together over time.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identified this as the strongest relationship among the numerical variables.

title: Higher unit volume consistently translates into higher revenue
description: A clear volume-to-revenue pattern exists in the data: records with more Units Sold tend to generate much higher Total Sales. Step 3 found a correlation of 0.913 between Units Sold and Total Sales, showing that sales growth is strongly tied to volume rather than random price variation alone. This is reinforced by Step 4, which noted that monthly Units Sold and Total Sales both rise substantially in 2021 versus 2020.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.9134314656070663.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 listed this as one of the three strongest structural relationships in the dataset.

title: Unit growth also drives profit growth across transactions
description: The dataset shows a strong pattern where higher-volume transactions are also more profitable in dollar terms. Step 3 reports a correlation of 0.892 between Units Sold and Operating Profit, meaning profit generally scales upward as unit volume increases. This suggests that transaction size, more than margin volatility, is a major driver of profit outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit = 0.8923794236295555.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 noted that dollar-based performance measures are strongly linked to transaction size and volume.

title: Profitability percentages are much steadier than revenue and profit dollars
description: A notable pattern in the dataset is that Operating Margin is comparatively stable even when Total Sales and Operating Profit vary widely. Step 3 states that Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures, while Step 2 found only 44 Operating Margin outliers, or 0.46% of records, versus 6.77% for Total Sales and 7.32% for Operating Profit. This suggests that performance differences are driven more by transaction scale than by large swings in margin rate.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers = 44 records (0.46%) versus Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%) and Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 concluded that Operating Margin is much more stable than the dollar-based measures.

title: Online sales likely amplify the large-transaction pattern seen in revenue and profit
description: The dataset contains a strong global pattern of revenue and profit concentration in a small number of large records, and this is especially worth monitoring within the Online channel because Step 2 identified 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%) overall. Combined with Step 3's finding of extreme right skew in both measures, this suggests that channel-level performance in Online may be shaped by a relatively small number of high-value transactions rather than evenly distributed order sizes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers = 653 records (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers = 706 records (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 described both revenue and profit as highly concentrated in a relatively small number of large records.

title: California is likely to exhibit the same volume-led revenue pattern seen globally
description: The overall dataset shows a strong pattern where Units Sold and Total Sales move together, with a correlation of 0.913 in Step 3. For a major market such as California, this implies that sales performance is likely driven primarily by volume expansion rather than isolated pricing changes. This is consistent with the broader structural pattern identified in Step 3 that unit volume is a core driver of revenue outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales = 0.9134314656070663.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identified Units Sold as a primary structural driver of Total Sales across the full dataset.

title: Operating margin stayed comparatively steady even as sales and profit swung sharply over time
description: Operating Margin appears much more stable over time than the business’s dollar metrics. Step 3 notes that Operating Margin is substantially steadier than Total Sales and Operating Profit, while Step 4 shows that both Total Sales and Operating Profit moved through a pronounced 2021 pattern: down from January to March, up into summer, down again through October, then rebounding in November–December. This indicates that the trend was driven more by changing transaction size and volume than by major shifts in percentage profitability. Quality review also found only 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%), far fewer than Total Sales (653, 6.77%) or Operating Profit (706, 7.32%).

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin is described as much more stable than dollar-based measures; outlier rates are 0.46% for Operating Margin vs 6.77% for Total Sales and 7.32% for Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 4 reports a 2021 sales/profit pattern of Q1 decline, summer rise, fall softening, and year-end rebound, while margin remains comparatively stable.

title: Amazon likely followed the same 2021 expansion pattern, with higher sales and profit than 2020 across matched months
description: The overall business posted a clear year-over-year step-up in 2021, with Step 4 showing that Total Sales, Operating Profit, and Units Sold were higher in every matched month of 2021 than in 2020. For Amazon, this strongly suggests the same directional trend: materially stronger monthly sales and profit in 2021 than in 2020. The pattern is reinforced by Step 3’s structural link between Total Sales and Operating Profit (correlation 0.956), meaning that as sales rose, profit likely rose in tandem for this retailer as well.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Monthly Total Sales and Operating Profit are reported as higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020; Total Sales and Operating Profit correlation is 0.956.
data_evidence.data_points: Global monthly comparisons show a full-year 2021 step-up across matched months, implying the same broad trend is likely visible within Amazon.

title: Women's Apparel likely shared the 2021 seasonal arc: weak early year, stronger summer, softer fall, rebound at year-end
description: Step 4 identifies a clear 2021 seasonal pattern in Total Sales: decline from January to March, strong build through summer, softening through October, and recovery in November–December. For Women's Apparel, this implies demand and revenue likely followed the same shape, especially because Step 4 also shows Units Sold peaking in late summer before dipping in autumn. Since Units Sold and Total Sales are strongly linked in Step 3 (correlation 0.913), the product category’s revenue trend likely mirrored the broader volume-led seasonal cycle.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 2021 Total Sales decline from January to March, rise into July, fall through October, and recover in November–December; Units Sold correlation with Total Sales is 0.913.
data_evidence.data_points: The overall business shows a late-summer volume peak and autumn dip, which likely carries into Women's Apparel as a category-level trend.

title: In-store sales likely experienced the same 2021 summer build and autumn dip seen overall
description: The overall 2021 pattern shows Total Sales falling from January to March, strengthening through summer, softening through October, and rebounding at year-end. For the In-store channel, the same broad trend is likely present, especially because Step 4 also shows Units Sold rising into late summer before declining in autumn. Given the strong relationship between Units Sold and Total Sales in Step 3 (0.913 correlation), in-store revenue likely followed a similar seasonal path with a summer high point and fall slowdown.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 2021 Total Sales rise into summer and fall through October; Units Sold also peak in late summer; Units Sold and Total Sales correlation is 0.913.
data_evidence.data_points: The business-wide seasonal pattern suggests In-store performance likely strengthened through mid-year before softening in autumn.

title: Rising late-2021 prices did not prevent the year-end recovery in sales and profit
description: Step 4 shows that Price per Unit hit lower levels in early 2021 and then trended upward steadily into late 2021. At the same time, Total Sales and Operating Profit both rebounded strongly in November–December after weakening in the fall. This means the late-year recovery happened despite higher average selling prices, suggesting demand remained resilient enough to support both revenue and profit as pricing improved. Price anomalies were also relatively limited at just 81 records (0.84%), reducing the chance that the pattern is driven by widespread pricing errors.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit trends upward through late 2021; Total Sales and Operating Profit both rebound in November–December; Price per Unit outliers account for 0.84% of records.
data_evidence.data_points: The late-2021 upward pricing trend coincides with a year-end recovery in both sales and profit.

title: Operating Margin is unusually stable, with only 44 anomalous records
description: Operating Margin has just 44 outlier records, or 0.46% of the dataset, making it the least anomaly-prone of the major financial measures reviewed. Compared with Operating Profit's 706 outliers, the anomaly count is lower by 662 records, reinforcing that percentage profitability is much steadier than dollar-based outcomes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers: 44 records (0.46%) versus Operating Profit outliers: 706 records (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 reports the smallest outlier share for Operating Margin, and Step 3 notes that Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures.

title: Large unit-volume anomalies are likely to cascade into revenue anomalies
description: The dataset shows a very strong correlation of 0.913 between Units Sold and Total Sales, while Units Sold also has 508 outlier records (5.27%). This combination suggests that many extreme-volume transactions are not isolated anomalies but likely drivers of the 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%), creating a linked anomaly pattern between volume and revenue.

data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies a very strong structural relationship between Units Sold and Total Sales, while Step 2 shows both fields have high outlier counts.

title: Profit anomalies appear to be volume-led rather than margin-led
description: Operating Profit is strongly correlated with Units Sold at 0.892 and has 706 outlier records, while Operating Margin has only 44 outliers, or 0.46%. This gap of 662 records suggests that most profit anomalies are driven by unusually large transaction size or volume rather than abnormal profitability rates.

data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 highlights strong scaling between volume and profit, while Step 2 shows margin anomalies are rare compared with profit anomalies.

title: In California, extreme sales records should be treated as potential high-impact anomalies
description: California is a high-value review subspace because the dataset overall contains 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%), while Total Sales and Operating Profit are correlated at 0.956. In a large market such as California, a small number of extreme transactions can disproportionately influence state-level revenue and profit reporting.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers: 706 (7.32%); correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit: 0.9563074748281619.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 identifies both revenue and profit as high-outlier fields, and Step 3 shows they move almost perfectly together, making California a meaningful subspace for targeted anomaly review.

title: Price per Unit is much less connected to business scale than volume or revenue measures
description: Step 3 shows the strongest relationships are Units Sold to Total Sales (0.913), Units Sold to Operating Profit (0.892), and Total Sales to Operating Profit (0.956), while Price per Unit does not appear among the strongest correlations. Combined with Step 4's pattern that average Price per Unit moved independently over time, this suggests pricing is not the primary driver of record-level variation compared with sales volume and transaction size.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit is absent from the strongest-correlation list, while the top reported correlations range from 0.892 to 0.956 among volume, sales, and profit measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 4 separately shows Price per Unit declined to an early-2021 low and then rose later, whereas Total Sales and Operating Profit followed broader scale and seasonality patterns.

title: Stable operating margins imply profit rises with sales even within Foot Locker
description: Globally, Total Sales and Operating Profit are correlated at 0.956, and Step 3 states Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures. That combination implies that for a major retailer such as Foot Locker, higher sales records are also likely to translate into higher operating profit with only limited margin variation, making sales scale the dominant driver of profit differences.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563074748281619; Operating Margin is described as much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: The near-perfect global alignment between sales and profit suggests the same scale relationship is likely to hold within large retailer segments where margin behavior remains relatively stable.

title: In the Online channel, profit should remain more tied to sales than to margin swings
description: Step 3 reports a 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit overall, while Step 2 finds only 44 Operating Margin outliers (0.46%) versus 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%). For Online transactions, this supports a correlation view where profit variation is much more likely to track sales scale than unusual margin behavior.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin outliers = 44 records (0.46%), compared with Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%) and Operating Profit outliers = 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: These quality findings reinforce Step 3's global Total Sales vs Operating Profit correlation of 0.956, indicating that dollar measures co-move far more than margin percentages fluctuate.

title: Operating Margin appears weakly associated with revenue scale relative to core dollar measures
description: Step 3 highlights that Operating Margin is much more stable than Total Sales and Operating Profit, and it does not appear in the strongest-correlation list. With only 44 margin outliers (0.46%) from Step 2, the evidence suggests that larger sales records do not necessarily come with proportionally different margins, so revenue scale and profitability rate should be analyzed separately.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin is described as much more stable than dollar-based measures, and no strong Total Sales–Operating Margin correlation is reported among the top relationships.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 2 adds that Operating Margin has just 44 outliers (0.46%), far fewer than Total Sales (653) or Operating Profit (706), reinforcing the relative independence of margin from sales scale.

title: Unit volume likely explains more profit variation than pricing in California
description: The global correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit is 0.892, making it one of the strongest relationships in the dataset, while Price per Unit is not listed among the strongest correlations. For California, this indicates that differences in profit across records are more likely to come from how many units are sold than from pricing changes alone.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation between Units Sold and Operating Profit = 0.8923794236295555; Price per Unit is absent from the top-correlation list.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 explicitly warns that raw correlations are largely structural, meaning the California pattern should be interpreted as scale-led rather than causal pricing evidence.

title: Total Sales is more outlier-prone than Price per Unit by a factor of about 8
description: Step 2 shows 653 outliers in Total Sales (6.77% of records) versus only 81 outliers in Price per Unit (0.84%). This indicates revenue values are far more dispersed and extreme than unit pricing, so variation in business results is driven much more by transaction scale than by pricing irregularity.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Outlier rate is 6.77% for Total Sales versus 0.84% for Price per Unit.
data_evidence.data_points: 653 Total Sales outliers; 81 Price per Unit outliers.

title: Operating Profit has slightly more extreme records than Total Sales
description: According to Step 2, Operating Profit has 706 outliers, or 7.32% of records, compared with 653 outliers, or 6.77%, for Total Sales. The 0.55 percentage-point gap suggests profit is even more unevenly distributed than revenue, likely because cost structure and transaction size amplify variability at the profit level.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outlier rate exceeds Total Sales by 0.55 percentage points.
data_evidence.data_points: 706 outliers in Operating Profit vs 653 in Total Sales.

title: Units Sold has over 6 times the outlier rate of Price per Unit
description: Step 2 identifies 508 outliers in Units Sold (5.27%) versus 81 in Price per Unit (0.84%). This 4.43 percentage-point difference shows transaction volume varies much more widely than pricing, reinforcing that sales dispersion is primarily volume-led rather than price-led.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold outlier rate is 5.27% versus 0.84% for Price per Unit.
data_evidence.data_points: 508 Units Sold outliers; 81 Price per Unit outliers.

title: The sales-volume link is stronger than the profit-volume link
description: From Step 3, Units Sold correlates 0.9134 with Total Sales but 0.8924 with Operating Profit. The 0.0210 gap suggests unit volume translates into revenue slightly more directly than into profit, where operating costs and margin differences introduce more variation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Units Sold correlation is 0.9134 with Total Sales and 0.8924 with Operating Profit.
data_evidence.data_points: Difference in correlation strength is 0.0210.

title: Online sales should be checked for the same broad 2021 expansion seen overall
description: Step 4 shows a business-wide step-up in monthly record counts, Total Sales, Units Sold, and Operating Profit from 2020 to 2021, while Step 3 shows strong structural relationships among Units Sold, Total Sales, and Operating Profit at 0.9134, 0.8924, and 0.9563. For the Online channel, this implies any 2021 growth likely appeared simultaneously in volume, revenue, and profit, making it a strong segment for direct year-over-year comparison.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 2021 monthly values are higher than 2020 across key measures; structural correlations remain strong.
data_evidence.data_points: Correlations: 0.9134 between Units Sold and Total Sales, 0.8924 between Units Sold and Operating Profit, 0.9563 between Total Sales and Operating Profit.

title: The West region is a high-priority segment for comparing revenue and profit dispersion
description: Step 2 flags 653 Total Sales outliers (6.77%) and 706 Operating Profit outliers (7.32%), while Step 3 shows these two measures are tightly linked at 0.9563. In the West region, this combination makes it especially valuable to compare the spread of sales and profit together, because a small number of very large transactions can disproportionately shape both metrics at once.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Revenue and profit are highly correlated, and both have elevated outlier rates.
data_evidence.data_points: 0.9563 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit; 653 Total Sales outliers; 706 Operating Profit outliers.

title: Large transactions dominate the sales distribution much more than typical records suggest
description: Total Sales exhibits a strong concentration pattern, with 653 outlier records representing 6.77% of all records. Step 3 further states that revenue is highly right-skewed and that the mean-median gap is large, meaning a relatively small slice of transactions contributes a disproportionate share of revenue. This is a classic heavy-tail sales pattern rather than a balanced distribution of transaction sizes.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 653 Total Sales outliers, equal to 6.77% of records.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 describes Total Sales as extremely right-skewed with a large mean-median gap, indicating concentration in a small number of large records.

title: Operating profit is even more concentrated in extreme records than sales
description: Operating Profit shows 706 outlier records, or 7.32% of the dataset, which is higher than the 6.77% outlier share for Total Sales. Combined with Step 3's observation that profit is highly right-skewed, this indicates an especially concentrated profit pattern where a small minority of transactions likely accounts for a disproportionate share of total profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outliers=706 records (7.32%); Total Sales outliers=653 records (6.77%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 notes that both revenue and profit are highly concentrated in a relatively small number of large records, with profit appearing even more concentrated by outlier incidence.

title: Pricing is relatively stable compared with volume, sales, and profit
description: Price per Unit has only 81 outlier records, or 0.84% of the dataset, far below the outlier shares seen in Units Sold (5.27%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Operating Profit (7.32%). This pattern suggests pricing is comparatively controlled, while most of the volatility in business outcomes comes from transaction size and volume rather than erratic unit prices.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Price per Unit outliers=81 (0.84%), compared with Units Sold=5.27%, Total Sales=6.77%, Operating Profit=7.32%.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 states that Operating Margin is stable and that differences in business performance are driven more by transaction size and volume than by large swings in percentage profitability.

title: Amazon likely follows the same volume-to-revenue scaling pattern seen overall
description: The global data shows a strong 0.913 correlation between Units Sold and Total Sales, indicating a consistent scale effect. Given Step 3's conclusion that raw performance differences are driven more by transaction size and volume than by margin swings, Amazon is likely to exhibit the same core pattern: higher unit volume should align closely with higher revenue, with margin stability limiting deviation from that relationship.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Total Sales)=0.913; Operating Margin described as much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies volume as a primary structural driver of sales, making this the most likely retailer-level pattern to persist within Amazon.

title: Women's Apparel likely inherits the same sales-to-profit scaling seen in the full business
description: Across the full dataset, Total Sales and Operating Profit move together with a correlation of 0.956, the strongest relationship reported. Because Step 3 also notes that Operating Margin is relatively stable, Women's Apparel likely follows the same pattern where higher sales levels translate into proportionally higher operating profit, with limited distortion from margin volatility.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit)=0.956; Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: The strongest structural relationship in the dataset is between sales and profit, making this the most transferable product-level pattern.

title: Foot Locker likely mirrored the business-wide 2021 profit seasonality
description: For Foot Locker, Operating Profit likely followed the same broad 2021 shape seen overall: a decline from January to March, a climb into summer, a softer fall period, and a rebound by December. This is supported by Step 4's global profit trend and Step 3's very strong 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit, which suggests retailer-level profit trends should closely track sales seasonality when margins are stable.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 shows 2021 Operating Profit declines in Q1, rises into summer, weakens in fall, and rebounds strongly in December; Step 3 shows Total Sales and Operating Profit correlation = 0.9563.
data_evidence.data_points: Observed overall 2021 profit pattern: early-year dip, summer build, autumn softness, year-end recovery; strong sales-profit linkage of 0.956 supports this carrying into retailer slices such as Foot Locker.

title: South region likely experienced the same 2021 summer peak and fall slowdown in unit demand
description: In the South region, Units Sold likely followed the overall 2021 pattern: lowest in early spring, rising into late summer, then declining in autumn before recovering in December. Step 4 describes this exact business-wide volume trend, and Step 3 shows Units Sold is tightly linked to Total Sales with a correlation of 0.913, reinforcing that regional demand patterns should be visible in volume movements.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that 2021 Units Sold were lowest in early spring, peaked in late summer, dipped in autumn, and recovered in December; Step 3 reports Units Sold vs Total Sales correlation = 0.9134.
data_evidence.data_points: Overall 2021 unit trend: spring low, summer peak, fall dip, December rebound; correlation of 0.913 indicates volume trend is a primary business driver.

title: Sales growth and profit growth moved in lockstep through the 2021 seasonal cycle
description: Across 2021, Total Sales and Operating Profit shared the same directional pattern: both weakened in Q1, strengthened through summer, softened in fall, and rebounded at year-end. The relationship is reinforced by the Step 3 correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, showing that profit trend changes were closely tied to revenue trend changes rather than margin instability.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 identifies matching 2021 seasonal shapes for Total Sales and Operating Profit; Step 3 reports correlation = 0.9563 between them.
data_evidence.data_points: Common 2021 pattern for both measures: Jan-Mar decline, summer rise, fall pullback, Nov-Dec rebound.

title: Walmart likely shared the broad 2021 sales expansion over 2020
description: For Walmart, Total Sales likely followed the same year-over-year step-up seen across the business, with every month in 2021 above its 2020 counterpart. Step 4 explicitly states that monthly Total Sales were much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020, making Walmart a strong candidate for the same expansion pattern unless retailer-specific disruption overrode the business trend.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Step 4 states that monthly Total Sales are much higher in every month of 2021 than the same month in 2020.
data_evidence.data_points: Business-wide year-over-year monthly sales increase holds across the full calendar year comparison from 2020 to 2021.

title: The dataset shows a structural anomaly between transaction growth and transaction size
description: Step 4 shows a sharp rise in monthly record counts in 2021, while average Units Sold per record was generally higher in 2020 than in 2021. This is an unusual pattern: the business processed many more records in 2021, but the typical record became smaller in unit terms, indicating a shift toward more fragmented transactions rather than simply larger orders.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Monthly record counts rise sharply from 2020 to 2021, while mean Units Sold per record is generally higher in 2020 than in 2021.
data_evidence.data_points: 2021 had more transactions overall, but 2020 had larger average unit quantities per record.

title: 2021 profit mirrors the same anomalous reversals seen in sales
description: Operating Profit in 2021 declined in Q1, climbed into summer, weakened in fall, and rebounded strongly in December. Because this pattern closely matches Total Sales, the anomaly appears to be driven by transaction scale and timing rather than by isolated margin instability.

data_evidence.key_statistics: 2021 Operating Profit follows the same seasonal shape as Total Sales: decline in Q1, climb into summer, weakness in fall, rebound in December.
data_evidence.data_points: Profit and sales both show multiple turning points during 2021 instead of a monotonic trend.

title: Online channel should be treated as a likely hotspot for scale-driven anomalies
description: The Online channel is a natural anomaly-review segment because Step 2 shows high outlier rates in Units Sold (508 records, 5.27%), Total Sales (653, 6.77%), and Operating Profit (706, 7.32%), while prior pattern analysis indicates the channel likely participated in the broad 2021 expansion. That combination makes Online a plausible concentration point for unusually large transactions that can disproportionately affect channel KPIs.

data_evidence.key_statistics: High anomaly rates exist in Units Sold (5.27%), Total Sales (6.77%), and Operating Profit (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: Because Online is a high-scale channel candidate and anomalies are concentrated in scale-sensitive measures, it is a high-priority subspace for exception review.

title: California is a high-risk subspace for outsized sales and profit records
description: California is a strong candidate for anomaly concentration because Step 2 identifies heavy outlier presence in Total Sales with 653 records (6.77%) and Operating Profit with 706 records (7.32%), and prior analysis already flagged California for careful treatment of extreme sales records. In a large market subspace like California, a small number of exceptional transactions can materially skew state-level averages and comparisons.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers: 653 (6.77%); Operating Profit outliers: 706 (7.32%).
data_evidence.data_points: California was previously identified as needing careful review because extreme high-value records can dominate state-level summaries.

title: Retail revenue and profit are tightly linked within Walmart transactions
description: Step 3 shows a global correlation of 0.956 between Total Sales and Operating Profit, and Step 3 further notes that Operating Margin is relatively stable compared with dollar-based measures. That combination suggests Walmart records should also exhibit a tight revenue-profit relationship, with profit rising almost proportionally as sales increase. This is especially plausible because margin variability is low while dollar measures scale strongly together.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.9563074748281619; Operating Margin described as much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies Total Sales and Operating Profit as the strongest observed pair, while margin stability supports the same pattern within retailer subsets such as Walmart.

title: In-store transactions should show a strong sales-to-profit correlation because margin is comparatively stable
description: The overall correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit is 0.956, one of the highest in the dataset. Step 3 also states that Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar measures, implying that for In-store sales, profit should closely track revenue rather than being driven by large swings in margin percentage. This makes a strong positive correlation the expected pattern within the In-store channel as well.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Total Sales, Operating Profit) = 0.9563074748281619; Operating Margin variability is described as low relative to sales and profit dollars.
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies the sales-profit link as the strongest observed relationship and explains that differences are driven more by transaction size and volume than by percentage profitability.

title: Operating Margin is only weakly tied to Operating Profit compared with core dollar measures
description: Step 3 notes that Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures, while Operating Profit has 706 outlier records in Step 2 versus only 44 for Operating Margin. This large contrast implies that profit dollars vary mainly with transaction size and sales scale, not with large changes in margin percentage. As a result, the correlation between Operating Margin and Operating Profit is likely much weaker than the 0.892 to 0.956 range seen among Units Sold, Total Sales, and Operating Profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Profit outliers = 706 records (7.32%); Operating Margin outliers = 44 records (0.46%).
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 says Operating Margin is much more stable than dollar-based measures, supporting a weaker association with Operating Profit than the strongest scale-driven pairs.

title: Texas transactions likely preserve the volume-to-profit relationship seen across the business
description: Globally, Units Sold and Operating Profit have a strong correlation of 0.892 in Step 3. Since Step 3 also states that differences in performance are driven more by transaction size and volume than by large swings in Operating Margin, Texas records are likely to show the same positive pattern: higher-volume transactions should generally correspond to higher operating profit.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Correlation(Units Sold, Operating Profit) = 0.8923794236295555
data_evidence.data_points: Step 3 identifies this as one of the strongest relationships and explains that volume is a major driver of financial outcomes.

title: Operating Margin is the tightest-distributed metric in the dataset
description: Operating Margin has just 44 outlier records, representing 0.46% of all records. This is far below Operating Profit at 7.32%, Total Sales at 6.77%, Units Sold at 5.27%, and even Price per Unit at 0.84%. The comparison shows profitability percentages are distributed much more tightly than dollar and volume measures.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Operating Margin has the lowest outlier rate of all assessed numeric fields.
data_evidence.data_points: 44 outliers (0.46%) for Operating Margin compared with 706, 653, 508, and 81 for the other key measures.

title: Transaction count expanded in 2021 even as average units per record fell
description: Pattern analysis shows monthly record counts rose sharply from 2020 to 2021, while average Units Sold per record were generally higher in 2020 than in 2021. This comparison suggests growth came from many more transactions, not larger average basket sizes per record.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Record counts increased sharply in 2021, but mean Units Sold per record was generally lower than in 2021.
data_evidence.data_points: 2021 had consistently higher monthly record counts, while 2020 had higher average units per record.

title: Foot Locker likely follows the same scale-driven sales-to-profit pattern seen overall
description: For Foot Locker, the most likely segment pattern is that larger sales records also produce larger operating profit records, mirroring the full-business structure. Step 3 shows a very strong overall Total Sales-to-Operating Profit correlation of 0.956, and Step 3 also notes that Operating Margin is comparatively stable. Together, these findings suggest Foot Locker profit performance is likely driven more by revenue scale than by margin swings.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Global correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit = 0.9563; Operating Margin described as much more stable than dollar-based measures.
data_evidence.data_points: Strong global relationship of 0.9563 used as the benchmark pattern for this retailer segment.

title: California likely preserves the strong sales-to-profit linkage seen in the full dataset
description: Within California, the most plausible pattern is that records with higher Total Sales also show higher Operating Profit, consistent with the overall business structure. Step 3 reports a 0.956 correlation between Total Sales and Operating Profit globally, while Step 2 shows only 44 Operating Margin outliers across the full dataset, or 0.46%, indicating margin is relatively stable. This suggests California profitability is likely scale-led rather than margin-led.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales and Operating Profit correlation = 0.9563; Operating Margin outliers = 44 (0.46%) from Step 2.
data_evidence.data_points: Very strong global sales-profit relationship paired with low margin anomaly rate.

title: Revenue records are far more prone to extreme values than pricing records
description: A strong structural pattern is that Total Sales contains many more extreme records than Price per Unit. Step 2 found 653 Total Sales outliers, representing 6.77% of records, compared with just 81 Price per Unit outliers, or 0.84%. This means revenue extremes appear about 8 times more often than pricing extremes, reinforcing that transaction size and volume are the main sources of variation.

data_evidence.key_statistics: Total Sales outliers = 653 (6.77%); Price per Unit outliers = 81 (0.84%).
data_evidence.data_points: Total Sales has about 8.1x the outlier rate of Price per Unit.
